resume:
Director of Clinical Operations | Multi-site ambulatory network

Summary: Operational leader for a 9-clinic ambulatory group, accountable for patient access, throughput, and quality outcomes across primary and specialty care.

Experience:
Director, Clinical Operations — Riverbend Health Partners (2020-present)
- Owned end-to-end scheduling redesign that cut new-patient wait time from 21 days to 6 days
- Led the EHR migration from Allscripts to Epic across all sites with zero downtime
- Managed a team of 140 across MA, RN, and front-desk roles

Senior Manager, Patient Access — Riverbend Health Partners (2018-2020)
- Ran the centralized call center; improved abandonment rate from 14% to 5%
- Note: the Epic migration was actually executed by the corporate IT PMO; I represented clinical stakeholders

Manager, Front Office Operations — Lakeshore Medical Group (2015-2018)
- Standardized intake workflows across 4 locations
- New-patient wait time work began here; the 21-to-6-day result spanned both employers and was largely a payer-mix change, not a process change

Education: BS Health Administration, MHA in progress
Certifications: Lean Six Sigma Green Belt (in progress)
jd: Riverbend's competitor, Cascade Care Network, seeks a Director of Clinical Operations to own access and throughput for a growing 12-site ambulatory footprint. Reporting to the VP of Operations, you will be accountable for third-next-available-appointment, no-show rate, room utilization, and provider productivity (wRVU per session). You will lead a cross-functional redesign of scheduling templates, partner with revenue cycle on front-end collections, and own the operational readiness for a planned Epic optimization wave. The role manages roughly 120 staff across four pods and requires demonstrated success reducing patient wait times, running a centralized access center, and navigating payer-mix shifts without sacrificing margin. CPHQ or Lean certification preferred. This is a hands-on leadership role, not a steering-committee seat.

history.q: Give me your current scope at Riverbend — sites, headcount, and the metrics you personally own.
Your resume says you cut new-patient wait time from 21 days to 6. Walk me through exactly what you changed.
You also note the wait-time result spanned two employers and was 'largely a payer-mix change.' Which is it — your process redesign or the payer mix?
Let's be precise: of the 15-day reduction, how many days came from scheduling changes you owned versus the payer shift?
On the Epic migration — your summary claims you led it, but a later line says corporate IT executed it. What did you actually own?
Tell me about the centralized call center. How did you get abandonment from 14% to 5%?
What was the no-show rate before and after your scheduling work?
Describe a time a redesign you led missed its target. What happened?
Who on your team was accountable for room utilization, and what did you do when it stalled?
Cascade has a planned Epic optimization wave. Given your migration was IT-led, why should we trust you to own operational readiness?
Let's go back to the no-show project specifically. It missed target by a wide margin. What went wrong and what did you personally do about it?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, the no-show project was set up to fail before I ever touched it. The scheduling vendor we were stuck with had a terrible reminder module, and the front-desk staff I inherited just wouldn't follow the scripts. Finance also pulled the budget for the text-reminder upgrade halfway through, so my hands were tied. And the providers kept double-booking against our templates, which blew up the no-show numbers no matter what we did. So the miss really wasn't on my side of the house.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the miss really wasn't on my side of the house

expected-yield: at least one concrete corrective action the candidate personally took once the no-show project was failing — a specific intervention, who they escalated to, and what measurable change resulted — rather than a list of external parties to blame
